Home / EV Home Charging / Charging Costs & Efficiency

How Much Charging Two EVs Costs a Household Per Month

Going two-EV? The real monthly charging math for a two-car electric household — combined kWh, rate strategies, and why the second EV costs less than the first.

EV Home Charging

By Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

July 8, 2026 6 min read

On this page

  1. The combined math
  2. Why the second EV is the cheaper one to add
  3. The rate strategy at two-EV scale
  4. Budgeting and tracking
  5. The bill you’ll actually see
  6. When the second EV changes the infrastructure math
  7. The one-charger-first strategy
  8. Quick answers
  9. A note on tracking two cars without the spreadsheet
  10. The bottom line

The second EV decision usually hinges on infrastructure questions — can one circuit handle both? — but the budget question deserves an equally clear answer: what does a fully electric two-car household actually pay per month for charging? Here’s the honest math, plus the pleasant surprise that the second EV is cheaper to add than the first was.

The combined math

Household charging cost is just each car’s miles ÷ efficiency × rate, summed — with charging losses included. Take a representative household: a commuter sedan (1,100 miles/month at 3.6 mi/kWh) and a family crossover (700 miles/month at 3.0 mi/kWh):

A two-EV household's monthly charging
MilesWall kWh (loss-adj.)@ $0.16 flat@ $0.10 off-peak
Commuter sedan1,100~342~$55~$34
Family crossover700~261~$42~$26
Household total1,800~603~$97/mo~$60/mo
Illustrative but realistic. Your miles, models, and rates move the totals — the method transfers.

So a typical fully-electric two-car household lands around $60–100 per month in home charging — for 1,800 miles of driving. The gas equivalent (say both cars averaging 26 MPG combined at $3.50/gallon) runs about $242/month. The two-EV household is saving roughly $145–180 every month on fuel alone, which is the number that belongs in the go-electric-on-the-second-car decision.

Why the second EV is the cheaper one to add

Counterintuitively, EV #2’s effective cost of charging is lower than EV #1’s was, for three stacking reasons:

🏗The infrastructure existsCircuit, charger, permit — paid for once. EV #2 rides the same setup via sharing or scheduling, often for $0 extra.
📉The rate plan is already optimizedThe TOU switch happened for EV #1 — the second car's kWh land straight into the discount window from day one.
🧠The habits are builtSchedules, charge limits, plug-in reflexes — household knowledge that transfers instantly to car #2.

The first EV carried the setup costs and the learning curve; the second one just plugs into all of it. Marginal cost of adding EV #2: its electricity, and usually nothing else.

The rate strategy at two-EV scale

Everything about off-peak charging matters roughly twice as much with two cars. In the worked example, the schedule saves ~$37/month — $440/year — which upgrades rate optimization from “nice habit” to “meaningful money.” Two-EV specifics worth noting:

Window capacity is rarely the issue. ~600 kWh/month is ~20 kWh/night combined — about 2 hours of one 40A circuit’s output. Even a 6-hour super-off-peak window holds both cars’ needs with room to spare, staggered or load-shared.

EV-specific plans get more attractive. The bigger your shiftable load, the more a deep overnight discount is worth and the easier it outweighs the peak premium on fixed household usage. Two-EV households are the plans’ ideal customers — re-run the comparison tool if you skipped the switch at one car.

Route by efficiency when convenient. A light habit with real returns: the efficient car takes the long solo errands, the big one does what it’s for. At two-EV mileage volumes, the habit is worth a few dollars every month for zero effort.

Budgeting and tracking

For a clean household number, skip per-car math and read totals: your charger app’s monthly kWh (or both apps’ sum) × your rate = the true, loss-inclusive household figure. Watch it for one summer and one winter month — the cold-weather swing at two-car scale is noticeable (often +20–35% on the charging line in a hard winter month) and knowing your seasonal range prevents budget surprises. If you reimburse work mileage or split costs in the household, per-car session logs give you the split without spreadsheet archaeology.

The bill you’ll actually see

Translating the household math onto the utility bill: your total kWh rises by both cars’ combined charging (roughly 600 kWh in the worked example — a visible jump), and on a TOU plan nearly all of it should appear in the off-peak or super-off-peak bucket. That bucket concentration is your monthly health check: two scheduled cars put a large, unmistakable block of usage in the discount window, and any meaningful charging kWh appearing in peak periods means one car’s schedule silently failed — with two cars, twice the schedules that can drift after software updates or power outages. The two-EV household’s five-minute seasonal bill check pays double what a single-car household’s does, because both the savings and the silent-failure stakes are doubled.

When the second EV changes the infrastructure math

The happy default — EV #2 rides the existing setup free — has exceptions worth budgeting honestly. If both cars are high-mileage, the one-circuit solutions may need upgrading to paired load-sharing chargers (a few hundred dollars plus installation) or, rarely, a second circuit (a real project, panel-dependent). If your panel was near capacity after EV #1, the load calculation for simultaneous charging may force the conversation earlier. Neither changes the operating economics — the $60–100/month figure holds regardless of how the electrons are divided — but the honest total-cost picture for a second EV includes a hardware line that ranges from $0 (scheduling on one charger) to four figures (new circuit on a tight panel), with most households landing at the cheap end.

The one-charger-first strategy

For households going two-EV gradually, a sensible sequencing note: don’t buy the second charger before living with the shared setup. Start both cars on the existing single charger with staggered schedules — for many households, the plug swap happens twice a week and never annoys anyone, and the second-charger money stays in your pocket. If after a month the swapping genuinely grates, buy the paired load-sharing unit with real knowledge of your pattern. The infrastructure can always grow; it can’t shrink. The same patience applies to circuit upgrades — let demonstrated overnight shortfalls, not showroom anxiety, justify the electrician.

Quick answers

Will two EVs strain my home’s electrical service? Usually no — managed properly, the combined overnight load fits typical panels. An electrician’s load calc settles edge cases.

Is it worth a second charger? Convenience, not economics — plug-swapping is free; paired load-sharing units end the swapping. The electricity cost is identical either way.

What about one EV + one PHEV? The gentlest version — the PHEV adds only $10–25/month of charging and can live on Level 1, barely touching the main setup.

A note on tracking two cars without the spreadsheet

If per-car costs matter to you (mileage reimbursement, teen-driver accountability, plain curiosity), resist the manual-log urge — the data already exists in three places. Each car’s app reports its energy added; the charger’s session history shows wall-side totals with timestamps; and on staggered schedules, the timestamps alone attribute every session to a car. Five minutes at month-end assembling those numbers beats any logbook, and after a few months the split is so stable you’ll stop checking. Households that need a hard per-car figure for taxes or reimbursement can add an energy-monitoring plug or use the charger’s per-session export — but for most, the apps’ built-in totals answer the question for free.

The bottom line

A two-EV household typically pays $60–100/month for all its driving energy at home — versus roughly $240+ for the same miles on gas. The second EV inherits paid-for infrastructure, an optimized rate, and practiced habits, making it the easiest EV adoption decision most households ever face: the savings double while the setup costs stay behind at car #1.

Educational content, not financial advice. Model your two-car scenario with real rates in the Sim-Lab.

Written by Louis F.

Electric Vehicle & Home Efficiency Specialist

A developer and digital creator dedicated to simplifying the intersection of electric vehicle ownership and home energy management.
